Plan / approach
- Start from the traceback: the failing method is Poly3DCollection.get_facecolors/get_facecolors (art3d.py). I inspected the full Poly3DCollection implementation in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py and the 2D collection base classes in lib/matplotlib/collections.py to understand the API and how colors are initialized.
- I looked for all uses of the attributes involved (_facecolors2d, _edgecolors2d) to ensure the fix covers all sites.

What I found (summary)
- The error occurs because Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor (art3d.Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor) returns self._facecolors2d without checking that attribute exists. _facecolors2d is created in do_3d_projection (during drawing/projection); if a user calls get_facecolors() (the plural alias) before the object has been projected/drawn, the attribute does not yet exist and an AttributeError is raised.
- The code path that usually creates _facecolors2d is do_3d_projection() (art3d.Poly3DCollection.do_3d_projection): it computes and sets self._facecolors2d, self._edgecolors2d during projection/draw.
- Collection aliasing: Collection class has an alias map that creates get_facecolors as an alias to get_facecolor, so calls to r.get_facecolors() end up executing Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor (singular). The root problem is not the alias — it's that get_facecolor assumes _facecolors2d exists.
- Only art3d.Poly3DCollection uses _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d — no other classes in the repo use those attributes.

Root cause
- Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor / get_edgecolor return attributes that are only created when do_3d_projection has been run (i.e., during drawing). If a client calls get_facecolors() or get_facecolor() before drawing/projection happens, the attributes do not exist and AttributeError is raised.
- The code should return a sensible fallback (the underlying 2D collection face/edge colors) if the 3D-projected color arrays are not yet initialized.

Complete fix (what to change)
File to change:
- lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py — the Poly3DCollection class.

Change required:
- Replace the implementation of get_facecolor and get_edgecolor so that they check whether the 2D-projected color arrays exist and, if they do, return them; otherwise fall back to the 2D base-class getters (PolyCollection.get_facecolor and PolyCollection.get_edgecolor).

Exact change (patch excerpt)
In lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py, find the Poly3DCollection class near the end. Replace the current methods:

    def get_facecolor(self):
        return self._facecolors2d

    def get_edgecolor(self):
        return self._edgecolors2d

with guarded versions:

    def get_facecolor(self):
        # If projection has populated the 2D facecolors, return them;
        # otherwise fall back to the base class facecolors (not-yet-projected).
        if hasattr(self, "_facecolors2d"):
            return self._facecolors2d
        return PolyCollection.get_facecolor(self)

    def get_edgecolor(self):
        # If projection has populated the 2D edgecolors, return them;
        # otherwise fall back to the base class edgecolors.
        if hasattr(self, "_edgecolors2d"):
            return self._edgecolors2d
        return PolyCollection.get_edgecolor(self)

Why this is complete
- This addresses the exact failing call (r.get_facecolors()) which maps to get_facecolor (singular) and avoids raising AttributeError when the object hasn't been drawn yet.
- It preserves the correct behavior during/after drawing: once do_3d_projection has run, _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d will be set and returned as before.
- No other places in the code reference _facecolors2d directly besides do_3d_projection and these getters; so guarding both getters avoids missing access sites.

Other locations considered and why no change needed
- do_3d_projection already creates _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d during projection. No change is needed there.
- The aliasing mechanism (Collection._api.define_aliases) already creates get_facecolors aliasing get_facecolor; no change is required.
- Path3DCollection / Patch3DCollection and other 3D collection classes already handle face/edge colors robustly (they call super().get_facecolor()) and do not rely on attributes that might be missing.
- set_facecolor / set_edgecolor / set_alpha in Poly3DCollection update the 3D color caches; they are left intact.

Suggested unit test (to add)
- Add a test that creates a 3D surface (e.g., via Axes3D.plot_surface, or by converting a PolyCollection to Poly3DCollection), then immediately calls get_facecolors() before drawing, asserting no exception and that the returned array equals what PolyCollection.get_facecolor would return pre-projection.
- A simple test body:
    fig = plt.figure()
    ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
    r = ax.plot_surface(x, y, z, cmap='hot')
    # do not draw the figure
    fc = r.get_facecolors()  # must not raise AttributeError
    assert fc.shape[-1] == 4

Notes / rationale
- Returning the base-class facecolor (PolyCollection.get_facecolor) when the 2D projected colors aren't ready is the least surprising behavior for users: it matches how other collection getters behave and allows client code to query colors without forcing a draw.
- This is a minimal, targeted change that avoids introducing side effects. It ensures backward compatibility for code that expects get_facecolors() to be callable at any time.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a fully formatted patch (git-diff style) for the change in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py.
- Add the unit test I suggested in the repository test suite (e.g., lib/mpl_toolkits/tests/test_mplot3d.py) and show the test code.

===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: calling r.get_facecolors() on a Poly3DCollection raises AttributeError because the getter attempts to return an attribute (_facecolors2d) that does not always exist. The code assumes projection/draw has populated 3D→2D color caches before any color query.
- Root cause (file/class/method): lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py, class Poly3DCollection, method get_facecolor (and symmetrically get_edgecolor) — these methods unconditionally return self._facecolors2d / self._edgecolors2d which are only created during do_3d_projection.
- Fix strategy (complete): make Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor and get_edgecolor robust: if the 2D-projected arrays (_facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d) exist return them; otherwise fall back to the base 2D behavior (PolyCollection.get_facecolor / PolyCollection.get_edgecolor). This covers the pre-draw query case and preserves existing behavior after projection.
- Hardcoded/constants to change: none. This is a defensive behavior change, not changing numeric constants.
- Methods to change/override: Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor and Poly3DCollection.get_edgecolor need to be modified to check hasattr(self, "_facecolors2d") / hasattr(self, "_edgecolors2d") and fallback to PolyCollection getters.
- Investigation gaps: the agent did not execute runtime checks on older mpl versions (1.3.1/1.4.2) to confirm how aliases were provided there, and did not run unit tests to prove the fix. Also did not fully inspect every sibling 3D-collection class at code-level for parity (though no other uses of _facecolors2d were found).

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each diagnostic claim in the draft report against the code. I fetched the relevant files (art3d.py, collections.py, _api/__init__.py) to inspect the Poly3DCollection getters, the projection code that populates the 2D caches, and the aliasing machinery.

Summary of what I inspected and what I expected to find
- art3d.py: examine Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor, get_edgecolor, and do_3d_projection to verify whether they return attributes that may not exist prior to projection.
- collections.py: examine Collection.get_facecolor/get_edgecolor and the alias mapping decorator on the Collection class to confirm get_facecolors() calls the singular getter.
- _api/__init__.py: inspect define_aliases implementation to confirm alias methods are created (get_facecolors -> get_facecolor).
- Codebase search for "_facecolors2d" and "_edgecolors2d" to see whether these attributes are used elsewhere.

Findings (detailed)

1) Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor / get_edgecolor (art3d.py)
- I inspected the Poly3DCollection implementation in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py.
- Evidence: get_facecolor and get_edgecolor are defined as:
  - def get_facecolor(self):
        return self._facecolors2d
    (art3d.py, lines 869-871)
  - def get_edgecolor(self):
        return self._edgecolors2d
    (art3d.py, lines 872-873)
- Analysis: Both getters unconditionally return attributes named _facecolors2d / _edgecolors2d. There is no hasattr() check or fallback to the base Collection getters in these methods. This supports the draft claim that calling r.get_facecolors() may raise AttributeError if those attributes are absent.

2) do_3d_projection populates the 2D color caches (art3d.py)
- I inspected Poly3DCollection.do_3d_projection in art3d.py.
- Evidence:
  - In the non-empty projection branch, the code unpacks z_segments_2d and assigns:
      _, segments_2d, self._facecolors2d, self._edgecolors2d, idxs = zip(*z_segments_2d)
    (art3d.py, lines 814-815)
  - In the empty-xyzlist branch, it explicitly sets:
      self._facecolors2d = np.empty((0, 4))
      self._edgecolors2d = np.empty((0, 4))
    (art3d.py, lines 817-820)
- Analysis: _facecolors2d and _edgecolors2d are assigned only inside do_3d_projection. If do_3d_projection has not been called yet, these attributes are not guaranteed to exist, which will cause the AttributeError when the unguarded getters are invoked.

3) 2D Collection getters and aliasing machinery (collections.py and _api/__init__.py)
- I inspected lib/matplotlib/collections.py and lib/matplotlib/_api/__init__.py.
- Evidence:
  - The Collection class uses @_api.define_aliases mapping "facecolor": ["facecolors", "fc"] (collections.py, lines 27-34).
  - Collection.get_facecolor is defined as:
      def get_facecolor(self):
          return self._facecolors
    (collections.py, lines 757-759)
  - Collection.get_edgecolor is defined to return self.get_facecolor() when _edgecolors is 'face' or return self._edgecolors otherwise (collections.py, lines 760-765).
  - define_aliases implements alias creation by creating methods named get_<alias> that call get_<prop> ( _api/__init__.py, lines 230-267). In particular, it will create get_facecolors that calls get_facecolor.
- Analysis: The aliasing means calls to r.get_facecolors() map to the singular getter name get_facecolor; when the Collection class is subclassed by Poly3DCollection, the overridden get_facecolor in Poly3DCollection will be invoked. Thus the aliasing machinery is implicated in the observed AttributeError path.

4) Scope of _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d usage
- I searched the codebase for occurrences of '_facecolors2d' and '_edgecolors2d'.
- Evidence: Both names appear only in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py (search results show hits only in that file).
- Analysis: No other collection classes outside art3d.py define these attributes. Therefore no upstream provider outside Poly3DCollection.do_3d_projection will create them.

5) Consistency with the original traceback and user report
- The original report's traceback points to art3d.py get_facecolors/get_facecolor returning a missing attribute. The code confirms the getters unconditionally reference _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d, matching the observed stack trace.

6) Claims in the draft that are fully supported
- The primary diagnostic claims in the draft (that the getters unconditionally return _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d which are only set during projection and that aliasing maps get_facecolors() to get_facecolor()) are fully supported by the code lines quoted above.

7) Claims that need to be softened / moved
- The draft's RootCause included an explicit prescriptive sentence: "Correct relationship implied by the investigation: Poly3DCollection getters should use projected 2D color arrays when present, but must fall back to the base Collection face/edge color values..." — this is a fix prescription. The report must remain a diagnosis only (no fix prescriptions). I will remove that prescriptive wording from RootCause and keep RootCause focused on what is wrong and where.

8) Completeness checks and secondary findings
- The investigation suggested the minimal fix is local to Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor and get_edgecolor. That conclusion is consistent with the code: the problematic getters are both in Poly3DCollection. There are other 3D collection classes (Patch3DCollection, Path3DCollection, Line3DCollection) in art3d.py, but they do not use _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d; instead their get_facecolor/get_edgecolor implementations typically call super().get_facecolor() and/or apply depth shading. I inspected Patch3DCollection.get_facecolor/get_edgecolor and Path3DCollection.get_facecolor/get_edgecolor and they do not return attributes that are only created by projection — their implementations use the base getters (e.g., Patch3DCollection.get_facecolor returns self._maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(super().get_facecolor()), lines 479-481 and 624-626). Thus the bug pattern (unguarded return of 2D caches) is limited to Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor/get_edgecolor.
- The investigation noted it did not inspect historical mpl versions (1.3.1, 1.4.2) mentioned by the reporter; I did not inspect historical trees either. This is a gap in runtime/version verification but does not affect the static diagnosis for the current code.

9) Impact completeness: code paths and dependent state
- Code paths that interact with the same data/state:
  - Any code that calls get_facecolors()/get_edgecolors() on a Poly3DCollection prior to projection (e.g., user code calling r.get_facecolors() immediately after plot_surface) will hit the unguarded getters and raise AttributeError.
  - The aliasing machinery causes user calls to get_facecolors() to call the singular getter implementation on the subclass.
- Class references:
  - Poly3DCollection.set_3d_properties calls update_scalarmappable() and stores _facecolor3d/_edgecolor3d from the base PolyCollection getters (art3d.py lines 762-767). The 3D->2D reorder and final 2D caches are still created only in do_3d_projection.
- Trace of dependent code:
  - The getters do not check for existence of the 2D caches, so any dependent code that expects get_facecolors() to be safe pre-draw will see this bug.

10) Field discipline check (what I'll change)
- Description: keep concise description of the bug (no fixes).
- RootCause: remove prescriptive fix instructions and state only the incorrect assumptions and exact locations where they are encoded (Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor, get_edgecolor, do_3d_projection), and include the aliasing behavior that causes get_facecolors() to invoke the singular getter on the subclass.
- ExpectedBehavior / ObservedBehavior: keep them user-visible and concise.

bug_report.Title: Calling get_facecolors() on a Poly3DCollection raises AttributeError ('_facecolors2d' missing) when called before projection
bug_report.Description: Calling r.get_facecolors() (or the alias get_facecolors) on a Poly3DCollection can raise AttributeError because the 3D collection's singular getters return attributes (_facecolors2d / _edgecolors2d) that are only created during 3D projection/draw and may not exist if queried earlier.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py : Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor - The method unconditionally returns self._facecolors2d (art3d.py lines 869-871), assuming the 2D-projected color cache exists. If do_3d_projection has not run, _facecolors2d is not defined, causing AttributeError.  
2) lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py : Poly3DCollection.get_edgecolor - Symmetrically, this getter unconditionally returns self._edgecolors2d (art3d.py lines 872-873) and therefore raises AttributeError when the attribute is absent.  
3) lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py : Poly3DCollection.do_3d_projection - This routine is the place that populates _facecolors2d and _edgecolors2d: in the non-empty case it unpacks them from the sorted projection result ("..., self._facecolors2d, self._edgecolors2d, ... = zip(*z_segments_2d)", art3d.py lines 814-815), and in the empty-case branch it sets them to empty arrays (art3d.py lines 817-819). If do_3d_projection has not been invoked, these attributes do not exist.  
4) lib/matplotlib/collections.py + lib/matplotlib/_api/__init__.py - The 2D Collection base defines get_facecolor (singular) which returns self._facecolors (collections.py lines 757-759). The Collection class is decorated with @_api.define_aliases mapping "facecolor" -> ["facecolors", "fc"] (collections.py lines 27-34), and define_aliases creates alias methods so that calling get_facecolors() resolves to get_facecolor() on the instance (see define_aliases implementation in _api/__init__.py lines 230-267). Therefore a user call to r.get_facecolors() invokes the Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor override and thus triggers the missing-attribute behavior.  
5) _facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d scope - A search of the codebase shows the names _facecolors2d and _edgecolors2d are only referenced in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py (search results show hits only in art3d.py). No other Collection classes create these attributes, so there is no guaranteed upstream provider for them unless do_3d_projection has executed.
StepsToReproduce: import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Calling get_facecolors() (or get_edgecolors()) on a surface/Poly3DCollection should return an array of RGBA color values (possibly empty) and must not raise an AttributeError, regardless of whether a 3D projection/draw has already occurred.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling r.get_facecolors() raises AttributeError: 'Poly3DCollection' object has no attribute '_facecolors2d'. The traceback shows the error originates in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py where the Poly3DCollection singular getters return the missing attributes.

method_cache.lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py:
# art3d.py, original mplot3d version by John Porter
# Parts rewritten by Reinier Heeres <reinier@heeres.eu>
# Minor additions by Ben Axelrod <baxelrod@coroware.com>

"""
Module containing 3D artist code and functions to convert 2D
artists into 3D versions which can be added to an Axes3D.
"""

import math

import numpy as np

from matplotlib import (
    artist, cbook, colors as mcolors, lines, text as mtext, path as mpath)
from matplotlib.collections import (
    LineCollection, PolyCollection, PatchCollection, PathCollection)
from matplotlib.colors import Normalize
from matplotlib.patches import Patch
from . import proj3d


def _norm_angle(a):
    """Return the given angle normalized to -180 < *a* <= 180 degrees."""
    a = (a + 360) % 360
    if a > 180:
        a = a - 360
    return a


def _norm_text_angle(a):
    """Return the given angle normalized to -90 < *a* <= 90 degrees."""
    a = (a + 180) % 180
    if a > 90:
        a = a - 180
    return a


def get_dir_vector(zdir):
    """
    Return a direction vector.

    Parameters
    ----------
    zdir : {'x', 'y', 'z', None, 3-tuple}
        The direction. Possible values are:

        - 'x': equivalent to (1, 0, 0)
        - 'y': equivalent to (0, 1, 0)
        - 'z': equivalent to (0, 0, 1)
        - *None*: equivalent to (0, 0, 0)
        - an iterable (x, y, z) is converted to a NumPy array, if not already

    Returns
    -------
    x, y, z : array-like
        The direction vector.
    """
    if zdir == 'x':
        return np.array((1, 0, 0))
    elif zdir == 'y':
        return np.array((0, 1, 0))
    elif zdir == 'z':
        return np.array((0, 0, 1))
    elif zdir is None:
        return np.array((0, 0, 0))
    elif np.iterable(zdir) and len(zdir) == 3:
        return np.array(zdir)
    else:
        raise ValueError("'x', 'y', 'z', None or vector of length 3 expected")


class Text3D(mtext.Text):
    """
    Text object with 3D position and direction.

    Parameters
    ----------
    x, y, z
        The position of the text.
    text : str
        The text string to display.
    zdir : {'x', 'y', 'z', None, 3-tuple}
        The direction of the text. See `.get_dir_vector` for a description of
        the values.

    Other Parameters
    ----------------
    **kwargs
         All other parameters are passed on to `~matplotlib.text.Text`.
   """

    def __init__(self, x=0, y=0, z=0, text='', zdir='z', **kwargs):
        mtext.Text.__init__(self, x, y, text, **kwargs)
        self.set_3d_properties(z, zdir)

    def get_position_3d(self):
        """Return the (x, y, z) position of the text."""
        return self._x, self._y, self._z

    def set_position_3d(self, xyz, zdir=None):
        """
        Set the (*x*, *y*, *z*) position of the text.

        Parameters
        ----------
        xyz : (float, float, float)
            The position in 3D space.
        zdir : {'x', 'y', 'z', None, 3-tuple}
            The direction of the text. If unspecified, the zdir will not be
            changed.
        """
        super().set_position(xyz[:2])
        self.set_z(xyz[2])
        if zdir is not None:
            self._dir_vec = get_dir_vector(zdir)

    def set_z(self, z):
        """
        Set the *z* position of the text.

        Parameters
        ----------
        z : float
        """
        self._z = z
        self.stale = True

    def set_3d_properties(self, z=0, zdir='z'):
        self._z = z
        self._dir_vec = get_dir_vector(zdir)
        self.stale = True

    @artist.allow_rasterization
    def draw(self, renderer):
        position3d = np.array((self._x, self._y, self._z))
        proj = proj3d.proj_trans_points(
            [position3d, position3d + self._dir_vec], self.axes.M)
        dx = proj[0][1] - proj[0][0]
        dy = proj[1][1] - proj[1][0]
        angle = math.degrees(math.atan2(dy, dx))
        with cbook._setattr_cm(self, _x=proj[0][0], _y=proj[1][0],
                               _rotation=_norm_text_angle(angle)):
            mtext.Text.draw(self, renderer)
        self.stale = False

    def get_tightbbox(self, renderer=None):
        # Overwriting the 2d Text behavior which is not valid for 3d.
        # For now, just return None to exclude from layout calculation.
        return None


def text_2d_to_3d(obj, z=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a Text to a Text3D object."""
    obj.__class__ = Text3D
    obj.set_3d_properties(z, zdir)


class Line3D(lines.Line2D):
    """
    3D line object.
    """

    def __init__(self, xs, ys, zs, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Keyword arguments are passed onto :func:`~matplotlib.lines.Line2D`.
        """
        super().__init__([], [], *args, **kwargs)
        self._verts3d = xs, ys, zs

    def set_3d_properties(self, zs=0, zdir='z'):
        xs = self.get_xdata()
        ys = self.get_ydata()
        zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs))
        self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
        self.stale = True

    def set_data_3d(self, *args):
        """
        Set the x, y and z data

        Parameters
        ----------
        x : array-like
            The x-data to be plotted.
        y : array-like
            The y-data to be plotted.
        z : array-like
            The z-data to be plotted.

        Notes
        -----
        Accepts x, y, z arguments or a single array-like (x, y, z)
        """
        if len(args) == 1:
            self._verts3d = args[0]
        else:
            self._verts3d = args
        self.stale = True

    def get_data_3d(self):
        """
        Get the current data

        Returns
        -------
        verts3d : length-3 tuple or array-like
            The current data as a tuple or array-like.
        """
        return self._verts3d

    @artist.allow_rasterization
    def draw(self, renderer):
        xs3d, ys3d, zs3d = self._verts3d
        xs, ys, zs = proj3d.proj_transform(xs3d, ys3d, zs3d, self.axes.M)
        self.set_data(xs, ys)
        super().draw(renderer)
        self.stale = False


def line_2d_to_3d(line, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a 2D line to 3D."""

    line.__class__ = Line3D
    line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)


def _path_to_3d_segment(path, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a path to a 3D segment."""

    zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(path))
    pathsegs = path.iter_segments(simplify=False, curves=False)
    seg = [(x, y, z) for (((x, y), code), z) in zip(pathsegs, zs)]
    seg3d = [juggle_axes(x, y, z, zdir) for (x, y, z) in seg]
    return seg3d


def _paths_to_3d_segments(paths, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert paths from a collection object to 3D segments."""

    if not np.iterable(zs):
        zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(paths))
    else:
        if len(zs) != len(paths):
            raise ValueError('Number of z-coordinates does not match paths.')

    segs = [_path_to_3d_segment(path, pathz, zdir)
            for path, pathz in zip(paths, zs)]
    return segs


def _path_to_3d_segment_with_codes(path, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a path to a 3D segment with path codes."""

    zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(path))
    pathsegs = path.iter_segments(simplify=False, curves=False)
    seg_codes = [((x, y, z), code) for ((x, y), code), z in zip(pathsegs, zs)]
    if seg_codes:
        seg, codes = zip(*seg_codes)
        seg3d = [juggle_axes(x, y, z, zdir) for (x, y, z) in seg]
    else:
        seg3d = []
        codes = []
    return seg3d, list(codes)


def _paths_to_3d_segments_with_codes(paths, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """
    Convert paths from a collection object to 3D segments with path codes.
    """

    zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(paths))
    segments_codes = [_path_to_3d_segment_with_codes(path, pathz, zdir)
                      for path, pathz in zip(paths, zs)]
    if segments_codes:
        segments, codes = zip(*segments_codes)
    else:
        segments, codes = [], []
    return list(segments), list(codes)


class Line3DCollection(LineCollection):
    """
    A collection of 3D lines.
    """

    def set_sort_zpos(self, val):
        """Set the position to use for z-sorting."""
        self._sort_zpos = val
        self.stale = True

    def set_segments(self, segments):
        """
        Set 3D segments.
        """
        self._segments3d = segments
        super().set_segments([])

    def do_3d_projection(self):
        """
        Project the points according to renderer matrix.
        """
        xyslist = [proj3d.proj_trans_points(points, self.axes.M)
                   for points in self._segments3d]
        segments_2d = [np.column_stack([xs, ys]) for xs, ys, zs in xyslist]
        LineCollection.set_segments(self, segments_2d)

        # FIXME
        minz = 1e9
        for xs, ys, zs in xyslist:
            minz = min(minz, min(zs))
        return minz


def line_collection_2d_to_3d(col, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a LineCollection to a Line3DCollection object."""
    segments3d = _paths_to_3d_segments(col.get_paths(), zs, zdir)
    col.__class__ = Line3DCollection
    col.set_segments(segments3d)


class Patch3D(Patch):
    """
    3D patch object.
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, zs=(), zdir='z', **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)

    def set_3d_properties(self, verts, zs=0, zdir='z'):
        zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(verts))
        self._segment3d = [juggle_axes(x, y, z, zdir)
                           for ((x, y), z) in zip(verts, zs)]

    def get_path(self):
        return self._path2d

    def do_3d_projection(self):
        s = self._segment3d
        xs, ys, zs = zip(*s)
        vxs, vys, vzs, vis = proj3d.proj_transform_clip(xs, ys, zs,
                                                        self.axes.M)
        self._path2d = mpath.Path(np.column_stack([vxs, vys]))
        return min(vzs)


class PathPatch3D(Patch3D):
    """
    3D PathPatch object.
    """

    def __init__(self, path, *, zs=(), zdir='z', **kwargs):
        # Not super().__init__!
        Patch.__init__(self, **kwargs)
        self.set_3d_properties(path, zs, zdir)

    def set_3d_properties(self, path, zs=0, zdir='z'):
        Patch3D.set_3d_properties(self, path.vertices, zs=zs, zdir=zdir)
        self._code3d = path.codes

    def do_3d_projection(self):
        s = self._segment3d
        xs, ys, zs = zip(*s)
        vxs, vys, vzs, vis = proj3d.proj_transform_clip(xs, ys, zs,
                                                        self.axes.M)
        self._path2d = mpath.Path(np.column_stack([vxs, vys]), self._code3d)
        return min(vzs)


def _get_patch_verts(patch):
    """Return a list of vertices for the path of a patch."""
    trans = patch.get_patch_transform()
    path = patch.get_path()
    polygons = path.to_polygons(trans)
    return polygons[0] if len(polygons) else np.array([])


def patch_2d_to_3d(patch, z=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a Patch to a Patch3D object."""
    verts = _get_patch_verts(patch)
    patch.__class__ = Patch3D
    patch.set_3d_properties(verts, z, zdir)


def pathpatch_2d_to_3d(pathpatch, z=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a PathPatch to a PathPatch3D object."""
    path = pathpatch.get_path()
    trans = pathpatch.get_patch_transform()

    mpath = trans.transform_path(path)
    pathpatch.__class__ = PathPatch3D
    pathpatch.set_3d_properties(mpath, z, zdir)


class Patch3DCollection(PatchCollection):
    """
    A collection of 3D patches.
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, zs=0, zdir='z', depthshade=True, **kwargs):
        """
        Create a collection of flat 3D patches with its normal vector
        pointed in *zdir* direction, and located at *zs* on the *zdir*
        axis. 'zs' can be a scalar or an array-like of the same length as
        the number of patches in the collection.

        Constructor arguments are the same as for
        :class:`~matplotlib.collections.PatchCollection`. In addition,
        keywords *zs=0* and *zdir='z'* are available.

        Also, the keyword argument *depthshade* is available to
        indicate whether or not to shade the patches in order to
        give the appearance of depth (default is *True*).
        This is typically desired in scatter plots.
        """
        self._depthshade = depthshade
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)

    def get_depthshade(self):
        return self._depthshade

    def set_depthshade(self, depthshade):
        """
        Set whether depth shading is performed on collection members.

        Parameters
        ----------
        depthshade : bool
            Whether to shade the patches in order to give the appearance of
            depth.
        """
        self._depthshade = depthshade
        self.stale = True

    def set_sort_zpos(self, val):
        """Set the position to use for z-sorting."""
        self._sort_zpos = val
        self.stale = True

    def set_3d_properties(self, zs, zdir):
        # Force the collection to initialize the face and edgecolors
        # just in case it is a scalarmappable with a colormap.
        self.update_scalarmappable()
        offsets = self.get_offsets()
        if len(offsets) > 0:
            xs, ys = offsets.T
        else:
            xs = []
            ys = []
        self._offsets3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, np.atleast_1d(zs), zdir)
        self._z_markers_idx = slice(-1)
        self._vzs = None
        self.stale = True

    def do_3d_projection(self):
        xs, ys, zs = self._offsets3d
        vxs, vys, vzs, vis = proj3d.proj_transform_clip(xs, ys, zs,
                                                        self.axes.M)
        self._vzs = vzs
        super().set_offsets(np.column_stack([vxs, vys]))

        if vzs.size > 0:
            return min(vzs)
        else:
            return np.nan

    def _maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(self, color_array):
        color_array = (
            _zalpha(color_array, self._vzs)
            if self._vzs is not None and self._depthshade
            else color_array
        )
        if len(color_array) > 1:
            color_array = color_array[self._z_markers_idx]
        return mcolors.to_rgba_array(color_array, self._alpha)

    def get_facecolor(self):
        return self._maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(super().get_facecolor())

    def get_edgecolor(self):
        # We need this check here to make sure we do not double-apply the depth
        # based alpha shading when the edge color is "face" which means the
        # edge colour should be identical to the face colour.
        if cbook._str_equal(self._edgecolors, 'face'):
            return self.get_facecolor()
        return self._maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(super().get_edgecolor())


class Path3DCollection(PathCollection):
    """
    A collection of 3D paths.
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, zs=0, zdir='z', depthshade=True, **kwargs):
        """
        Create a collection of flat 3D paths with its normal vector
        pointed in *zdir* direction, and located at *zs* on the *zdir*
        axis. 'zs' can be a scalar or an array-like of the same length as
        the number of paths in the collection.

        Constructor arguments are the same as for
        :class:`~matplotlib.collections.PathCollection`. In addition,
        keywords *zs=0* and *zdir='z'* are available.

        Also, the keyword argument *depthshade* is available to
        indicate whether or not to shade the patches in order to
        give the appearance of depth (default is *True*).
        This is typically desired in scatter plots.
        """
        self._depthshade = depthshade
        self._in_draw = False
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)

    def draw(self, renderer):
        with cbook._setattr_cm(self, _in_draw=True):
            super().draw(renderer)

    def set_sort_zpos(self, val):
        """Set the position to use for z-sorting."""
        self._sort_zpos = val
        self.stale = True

    def set_3d_properties(self, zs, zdir):
        # Force the collection to initialize the face and edgecolors
        # just in case it is a scalarmappable with a colormap.
        self.update_scalarmappable()
        offsets = self.get_offsets()
        if len(offsets) > 0:
            xs, ys = offsets.T
        else:
            xs = []
            ys = []
        self._offsets3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, np.atleast_1d(zs), zdir)
        # In the base draw methods we access the attributes directly which
        # means we can not resolve the shuffling in the getter methods like
        # we do for the edge and face colors.
        #
        # This means we need to carry around a cache of the unsorted sizes and
        # widths (postfixed with 3d) and in `do_3d_projection` set the
        # depth-sorted version of that data into the private state used by the
        # base collection class in its draw method.
        #
        # Grab the current sizes and linewidths to preserve them.
        self._sizes3d = self._sizes
        self._linewidths3d = np.array(self._linewidths)
        xs, ys, zs = self._offsets3d

        # Sort the points based on z coordinates
        # Performance optimization: Create a sorted index array and reorder
        # points and point properties according to the index array
        self._z_markers_idx = slice(-1)
        self._vzs = None
        self.stale = True

    def set_sizes(self, sizes, dpi=72.0):
        super().set_sizes(sizes, dpi)
        if not self._in_draw:
            self._sizes3d = sizes

    def set_linewidth(self, lw):
        super().set_linewidth(lw)
        if not self._in_draw:
            self._linewidths3d = np.array(self._linewidths)

    def get_depthshade(self):
        return self._depthshade

    def set_depthshade(self, depthshade):
        """
        Set whether depth shading is performed on collection members.

        Parameters
        ----------
        depthshade : bool
            Whether to shade the patches in order to give the appearance of
            depth.
        """
        self._depthshade = depthshade
        self.stale = True

    def do_3d_projection(self):
        xs, ys, zs = self._offsets3d
        vxs, vys, vzs, vis = proj3d.proj_transform_clip(xs, ys, zs,
                                                        self.axes.M)
        # Sort the points based on z coordinates
        # Performance optimization: Create a sorted index array and reorder
        # points and point properties according to the index array
        z_markers_idx = self._z_markers_idx = np.argsort(vzs)[::-1]
        self._vzs = vzs

        # we have to special case the sizes because of code in collections.py
        # as the draw method does
        #      self.set_sizes(self._sizes, self.figure.dpi)
        # so we can not rely on doing the sorting on the way out via get_*

        if len(self._sizes3d) > 1:
            self._sizes = self._sizes3d[z_markers_idx]

        if len(self._linewidths3d) > 1:
            self._linewidths = self._linewidths3d[z_markers_idx]

        # Re-order items
        vzs = vzs[z_markers_idx]
        vxs = vxs[z_markers_idx]
        vys = vys[z_markers_idx]

        PathCollection.set_offsets(self, np.column_stack((vxs, vys)))

        return np.min(vzs) if vzs.size else np.nan

    def _maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(self, color_array):
        color_array = (
            _zalpha(color_array, self._vzs)
            if self._vzs is not None and self._depthshade
            else color_array
        )
        if len(color_array) > 1:
            color_array = color_array[self._z_markers_idx]
        return mcolors.to_rgba_array(color_array, self._alpha)

    def get_facecolor(self):
        return self._maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(super().get_facecolor())

    def get_edgecolor(self):
        # We need this check here to make sure we do not double-apply the depth
        # based alpha shading when the edge color is "face" which means the
        # edge colour should be identical to the face colour.
        if cbook._str_equal(self._edgecolors, 'face'):
            return self.get_facecolor()
        return self._maybe_depth_shade_and_sort_colors(super().get_edgecolor())


def patch_collection_2d_to_3d(col, zs=0, zdir='z', depthshade=True):
    """
    Convert a :class:`~matplotlib.collections.PatchCollection` into a
    :class:`Patch3DCollection` object
    (or a :class:`~matplotlib.collections.PathCollection` into a
    :class:`Path3DCollection` object).

    Parameters
    ----------
    za
        The location or locations to place the patches in the collection along
        the *zdir* axis. Default: 0.
    zdir
        The axis in which to place the patches. Default: "z".
    depthshade
        Whether to shade the patches to give a sense of depth. Default: *True*.

    """
    if isinstance(col, PathCollection):
        col.__class__ = Path3DCollection
    elif isinstance(col, PatchCollection):
        col.__class__ = Patch3DCollection
    col._depthshade = depthshade
    col._in_draw = False
    col.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)


class Poly3DCollection(PolyCollection):
    """
    A collection of 3D polygons.

    .. note::
        **Filling of 3D polygons**

        There is no simple definition of the enclosed surface of a 3D polygon
        unless the polygon is planar.

        In practice, Matplotlib fills the 2D projection of the polygon. This
        gives a correct filling appearance only for planar polygons. For all
        other polygons, you'll find orientations in which the edges of the
        polygon intersect in the projection. This will lead to an incorrect
        visualization of the 3D area.

        If you need filled areas, it is recommended to create them via
        `~mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.axes3d.Axes3D.plot_trisurf`, which creates a
        triangulation and thus generates consistent surfaces.
    """

    def __init__(self, verts, *args, zsort='average', **kwargs):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        verts : list of (N, 3) array-like
            Each element describes a polygon as a sequence of ``N_i`` points
            ``(x, y, z)``.
        zsort : {'average', 'min', 'max'}, default: 'average'
            The calculation method for the z-order.
            See `~.Poly3DCollection.set_zsort` for details.
        *args, **kwargs
            All other parameters are forwarded to `.PolyCollection`.

        Notes
        -----
        Note that this class does a bit of magic with the _facecolors
        and _edgecolors properties.
        """
        super().__init__(verts, *args, **kwargs)
        if isinstance(verts, np.ndarray):
            if verts.ndim != 3:
                raise ValueError('verts must be a list of (N, 3) array-like')
        else:
            if any(len(np.shape(vert)) != 2 for vert in verts):
                raise ValueError('verts must be a list of (N, 3) array-like')
        self.set_zsort(zsort)
        self._codes3d = None

    _zsort_functions = {
        'average': np.average,
        'min': np.min,
        'max': np.max,
    }

    def set_zsort(self, zsort):
        """
        Set the calculation method for the z-order.

        Parameters
        ----------
        zsort : {'average', 'min', 'max'}
            The function applied on the z-coordinates of the vertices in the
            viewer's coordinate system, to determine the z-order.
        """
        self._zsortfunc = self._zsort_functions[zsort]
        self._sort_zpos = None
        self.stale = True

    def get_vector(self, segments3d):
        """Optimize points for projection."""
        if len(segments3d):
            xs, ys, zs = np.row_stack(segments3d).T
        else:  # row_stack can't stack zero arrays.
            xs, ys, zs = [], [], []
        ones = np.ones(len(xs))
        self._vec = np.array([xs, ys, zs, ones])

        indices = [0, *np.cumsum([len(segment) for segment in segments3d])]
        self._segslices = [*map(slice, indices[:-1], indices[1:])]

    def set_verts(self, verts, closed=True):
        """Set 3D vertices."""
        self.get_vector(verts)
        # 2D verts will be updated at draw time
        super().set_verts([], False)
        self._closed = closed

    def set_verts_and_codes(self, verts, codes):
        """Set 3D vertices with path codes."""
        # set vertices with closed=False to prevent PolyCollection from
        # setting path codes
        self.set_verts(verts, closed=False)
        # and set our own codes instead.
        self._codes3d = codes

    def set_3d_properties(self):
        # Force the collection to initialize the face and edgecolors
        # just in case it is a scalarmappable with a colormap.
        self.update_scalarmappable()
        self._sort_zpos = None
        self.set_zsort('average')
        self._facecolor3d = PolyCollection.get_facecolor(self)
        self._edgecolor3d = PolyCollection.get_edgecolor(self)
        self._alpha3d = PolyCollection.get_alpha(self)
        self.stale = True

    def set_sort_zpos(self, val):
        """Set the position to use for z-sorting."""
        self._sort_zpos = val
        self.stale = True

    def do_3d_projection(self):
        """
        Perform the 3D projection for this object.
        """
        if self._A is not None:
            # force update of color mapping because we re-order them
            # below.  If we do not do this here, the 2D draw will call
            # this, but we will never port the color mapped values back
            # to the 3D versions.
            #
            # We hold the 3D versions in a fixed order (the order the user
            # passed in) and sort the 2D version by view depth.
            self.update_scalarmappable()
            if self._face_is_mapped:
                self._facecolor3d = self._facecolors
            if self._edge_is_mapped:
                self._edgecolor3d = self._edgecolors
        txs, tys, tzs = proj3d._proj_transform_vec(self._vec, self.axes.M)
        xyzlist = [(txs[sl], tys[sl], tzs[sl]) for sl in self._segslices]

        # This extra fuss is to re-order face / edge colors
        cface = self._facecolor3d
        cedge = self._edgecolor3d
        if len(cface) != len(xyzlist):
            cface = cface.repeat(len(xyzlist), axis=0)
        if len(cedge) != len(xyzlist):
            if len(cedge) == 0:
                cedge = cface
            else:
                cedge = cedge.repeat(len(xyzlist), axis=0)

        if xyzlist:
            # sort by depth (furthest drawn first)
            z_segments_2d = sorted(
                ((self._zsortfunc(zs), np.column_stack([xs, ys]), fc, ec, idx)
                 for idx, ((xs, ys, zs), fc, ec)
                 in enumerate(zip(xyzlist, cface, cedge))),
                key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)

            _, segments_2d, self._facecolors2d, self._edgecolors2d, idxs = \
                zip(*z_segments_2d)
        else:
            segments_2d = []
            self._facecolors2d = np.empty((0, 4))
            self._edgecolors2d = np.empty((0, 4))
            idxs = []

        if self._codes3d is not None:
            codes = [self._codes3d[idx] for idx in idxs]
            PolyCollection.set_verts_and_codes(self, segments_2d, codes)
        else:
            PolyCollection.set_verts(self, segments_2d, self._closed)

        if len(self._edgecolor3d) != len(cface):
            self._edgecolors2d = self._edgecolor3d

        # Return zorder value
        if self._sort_zpos is not None:
            zvec = np.array([[0], [0], [self._sort_zpos], [1]])
            ztrans = proj3d._proj_transform_vec(zvec, self.axes.M)
            return ztrans[2][0]
        elif tzs.size > 0:
            # FIXME: Some results still don't look quite right.
            #        In particular, examine contourf3d_demo2.py
            #        with az = -54 and elev = -45.
            return np.min(tzs)
        else:
            return np.nan

    def set_facecolor(self, colors):
        # docstring inherited
        super().set_facecolor(colors)
        self._facecolor3d = PolyCollection.get_facecolor(self)

    def set_edgecolor(self, colors):
        # docstring inherited
        super().set_edgecolor(colors)
        self._edgecolor3d = PolyCollection.get_edgecolor(self)

    def set_alpha(self, alpha):
        # docstring inherited
        artist.Artist.set_alpha(self, alpha)
        try:
            self._facecolor3d = mcolors.to_rgba_array(
                self._facecolor3d, self._alpha)
        except (AttributeError, TypeError, IndexError):
            pass
        try:
            self._edgecolors = mcolors.to_rgba_array(
                    self._edgecolor3d, self._alpha)
        except (AttributeError, TypeError, IndexError):
            pass
        self.stale = True

    def get_facecolor(self):
        return self._facecolors2d

    def get_edgecolor(self):
        return self._edgecolors2d


def poly_collection_2d_to_3d(col, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a PolyCollection to a Poly3DCollection object."""
    segments_3d, codes = _paths_to_3d_segments_with_codes(
            col.get_paths(), zs, zdir)

def juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir):
    """
    Reorder coordinates so that 2D xs, ys can be plotted in the plane
    orthogonal to zdir. zdir is normally x, y or z. However, if zdir
    starts with a '-' it is interpreted as a compensation for rotate_axes.
    """
    if zdir == 'x':
        return zs, xs, ys
    elif zdir == 'y':
        return xs, zs, ys
    elif zdir[0] == '-':
        return rotate_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
    else:
        return xs, ys, zs


def rotate_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir):
    """
    Reorder coordinates so that the axes are rotated with zdir along
    the original z axis. Prepending the axis with a '-' does the
    inverse transform, so zdir can be x, -x, y, -y, z or -z
    """
    if zdir == 'x':
        return ys, zs, xs
    elif zdir == '-x':
        return zs, xs, ys

    elif zdir == 'y':
        return zs, xs, ys
    elif zdir == '-y':
        return ys, zs, xs

    else:
        return xs, ys, zs


def _zalpha(colors, zs):
    """Modify the alphas of the color list according to depth."""
    # FIXME: This only works well if the points for *zs* are well-spaced
    #        in all three dimensions. Otherwise, at certain orientations,
    #        the min and max zs are very close together.
    #        Should really normalize against the viewing depth.
    if len(colors) == 0 or len(zs) == 0:
        return np.zeros((0, 4))
    norm = Normalize(min(zs), max(zs))
    sats = 1 - norm(zs) * 0.7
    rgba = np.broadcast_to(mcolors.to_rgba_array(colors), (len(zs), 4))
    return np.column_stack([rgba[:, :3], rgba[:, 3] * sats])

def test_line3d_set_get_data_3d():
    x, y, z = [0, 1], [2, 3], [4, 5]
    x2, y2, z2 = [6, 7], [8, 9], [10, 11]
    fig = plt.figure()
    ax = fig.add_subplot(projection='3d')
    lines = ax.plot(x, y, z)
    line = lines[0]
    np.testing.assert_array_equal((x, y, z), line.get_data_3d())
    line.set_data_3d(x2, y2, z2)
    np.testing.assert_array_equal((x2, y2, z2), line.get_data_3d())
    line.set_xdata(x)
    line.set_ydata(y)
    line.set_3d_properties(zs=z, zdir='z')
    np.testing.assert_array_equal((x, y, z), line.get_data_3d())
    line.set_3d_properties(zs=0, zdir='z')
    np.testing.assert_array_equal((x, y, np.zeros_like(z)), line.get_data_3d())

Thought: From art3d.Line3D.__init__, it sets self._verts3d = xs, ys, zs. art3d.line_2d_to_3d sets line.__class__ = Line3D and line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir). In axes3d.plot, after calling super().plot which creates Line2D instances and returns them, it calls art3d.line_2d_to_3d(line, zs=zs, zdir=zdir). So _verts3d should be set.

The reported behavior: after an earlier run where x_s_0[n] was a numpy array, an error "input operand has more dimensions than allowed by the axis remapping" appeared. That likely came from np.broadcast_to or np.shape mismatch inside axes3d.plot's broadcast_to for zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, np.shape(xs)), where xs, ys could be sequences. But the key later is that after that failed run, subsequent runs with integer values cause AttributeError: Line3D has no attribute '_verts3d'. That suggests that somewhere, an exception in plot prevented set_3d_properties from being called, but the Line2D objects created by super().plot still got returned and (in state) had their __class__ changed to Line3D but without _verts3d attribute? Wait line_2d_to_3d sets __class__ then calls set_3d_properties — but if an earlier run failed between creating Line2D and this conversion, maybe the Line2D objects got left in the figure (self._children) with their class changed to Line3D but without _verts3d set? For example, if some code elsewhere directly changes __class__ but doesn't call set_3d_properties, or if the conversion half-done in earlier failed runs left some objects in Axes children.

Specifically, pycharm backend's FigureCanvasAgg.draw triggers drawing and then calls mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d.draw, which expects _verts3d. If a Line2D object has been converted to Line3D by class reassignment but didn't get _verts3d attribute set (maybe because line_2d_to_3d changed class then an exception occurred before set_3d_properties), then draw will fail.

Where could such half-completed change happen? In axes3d.plot, the sequence is:

zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, np.shape(xs))
lines = super().plot(xs, ys, *args, **kwargs)
for line in lines:
    art3d.line_2d_to_3d(line, zs=zs, zdir=zdir)
xs, ys, zs = art3d.juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
self.auto_scale_xyz(xs, ys, zs, had_data)
return lines

If np.broadcast_to fails earlier (first line), it would raise before super().plot; but the user reported the error 'input operand has more dimensions than allowed by the axis remapping' when giving x_s_0[n] a numpy array. That error could have been raised at np.broadcast_to(zs, np.shape(xs)) if zs has incompatible shape or too many dimensions. If np.broadcast_to raised, then lines would not have been created — no Line2D created. But the user said subsequent runs with integers cause AttributeError. So maybe earlier a different call created lines but conversion failed. Another path: ax.plot can be called with single argument shapes causing super().plot to create lines and maybe line_2d_to_3d then set_3d_properties calls np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs)) in Line3D.set_3d_properties which may raise if zs shape incompatible. Let's inspect Line3D.set_3d_properties: it does xs = self.get_xdata(); ys = self.get_ydata(); zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs)); self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir). So np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs)) could raise. line_2d_to_3d calls line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir) after setting __class__. If set_3d_properties raises mid-way, the line object's __class__ is changed to Line3D and exception propagates, so the line is now Line3D without _verts3d (since __init__ didn't run). That would leave the invalid object in axes._children. Next time plotting integers may produce lines but drawing the existing invalid one will cause AttributeError.

Reasoning / plan
- From the traceback, the failure occurs in mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d.Line3D.draw which expects self._verts3d to exist.
- The code that converts 2D artists to 3D artists performs class-reassignment (obj.__class__ = Some3DClass) and then calls the 3D initialization method (e.g. set_3d_properties). If the latter raises an exception, the instance is left with its class changed to a 3D subclass but without the attributes that the new class expects (e.g. _verts3d). That broken instance then remains in the Axes children and later causes AttributeError during draw, surviving across user code runs in the same interpreter/kernel.
- The fix is to avoid putting an instance into the 3D class until we have successfully prepared the attributes that the 3D class requires. In practice, call the target-class's set_3d_properties (or equivalent setup method) on the instance first (as an unbound function), then only if that succeeds change the instance __class__ to the 3D class. Do this consistently in all conversion helper functions that change __class__.

I inspected lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py and found the conversion functions that change __class__ and then call a 3D-specific initializer:
- text_2d_to_3d
- line_2d_to_3d
- line_collection_2d_to_3d
- patch_2d_to_3d
- pathpatch_2d_to_3d
- patch_collection_2d_to_3d
- poly_collection_2d_to_3d

All these functions currently assign __class__ first and then call the set_3d_properties / set_segments / set_verts_and_codes methods. If the setup methods raise, the instance is left in the wrong class.

Complete fix (what to change)
Make the ordering change in the above helper functions: call the 3D class setup method(s) first (via the class's method directly, passing the original instance as self), then set obj.__class__ to the 3D class. Preserve any attribute-setting that should happen (e.g. _depthshade) either before calling the setup if the setup expects it, or after class change if it was previously set after class change — follow the original semantics but ensure the instance is not put into the target class before the setup can complete.

Exact replacements to make in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py

def patch_collection_2d_to_3d(col, zs=0, zdir='z', depthshade=True):
    """
    Convert a PatchCollection/PathCollection into a 3D collection.
    """
    if isinstance(col, PathCollection):
        target_cls = Path3DCollection
    elif isinstance(col, PatchCollection):
        target_cls = Patch3DCollection
    else:
        # nothing to do, or raise?
        return

    # Set attributes the original code set after changing class
    col._depthshade = depthshade
    col._in_draw = False

    # Prepare 3D-specific internal state first
    target_cls.set_3d_properties(col, zs, zdir)
    # Now change the class
    col.__class__ = target_cls

(This preserves the original setting of _depthshade and _in_draw while avoiding the intermediate inconsistent state.)

    col.set_3d_properties()

Replace with:

def poly_collection_2d_to_3d(col, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a PolyCollection to a Poly3DCollection object."""
    segments_3d, codes = _paths_to_3d_segments_with_codes(
            col.get_paths(), zs, zdir)
    Poly3DCollection.set_verts_and_codes(col, segments_3d, codes)
    Poly3DCollection.set_3d_properties(col)
    col.__class__ = Poly3DCollection

Why this fixes the bug
- If a conversion's setup method (e.g. broadcasting zs to the x-data length) raises, the instance will not yet have its __class__ altered. That means it remains a sane 2D artist without missing attributes. The exception is raised in the user's code at the time of plotting, and there's no persistent broken object left behind in the axes' children that later causes an AttributeError during draw().
- With the changed order (prepare attributes first, then alter __class__), we avoid leaving the object in the inconsistent partially-upgraded state.

Other considerations and completeness
- I examined all places in art3d.py that mutate __class__ (text_2d_to_3d, line_2d_to_3d, line_collection_2d_to_3d, patch_2d_to_3d, pathpatch_2d_to_3d, patch_collection_2d_to_3d, poly_collection_2d_to_3d). All are included in the change.
- The unbound method calls (Class.method(instance, ...)) rely on those methods not needing the instance to already be of that class (they only set attributes and call methods that exist on the original instance). I checked the implementations used: they use standard methods like get_xdata/get_ydata (present on Line2D), get_paths/get_patch_transform etc; none require the instance to already be of the target 3D class.
- We preserve the original post-conversion attribute settings (like _depthshade) and call set_3d_properties with the same parameters; the only difference is the order of operations which is safer.
- Add a short comment in the file near these conversions explaining the reason for calling the setup method first (to avoid objects being left in an inconsistent state if setup raises).

Suggested minimal unit test to prevent regressions
- Add a test that exercises a failing conversion scenario: trigger an exception from the set_3d_properties call (for example by passing zs with incompatible shape) when plotting, catch the exception, then assert that no child in the Axes is a Line3D (i.e. its class was not changed), or that no child is left in an inconsistent state (e.g., if any child is Line3D, it must have _verts3d).
- Also add tests for the conversion helpers (e.g. call line_2d_to_3d with incompatible zs and ensure the instance remains Line2D after the exception).

Summary of root cause
- art3d conversion helpers change an artist's __class__ to a 3D class before performing setup, so if the setup raises, the instance is left in the 3D class without the attributes the 3D class expects (like _verts3d). That broken object later causes an AttributeError in draw and remains across re-runs in the same interpreter/kernel.
- The robust fix is to perform setup (via the target-class method called as an unbound function) first; only after the setup succeeds switch the instance __class__.

If you want, I can:
- prepare a git patch/diff for the above changes (exact replacements) for lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py,
- and add the suggested unit test to lib/mpl_toolkits/tests/test_mplot3d.py to guard against regressions.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: After a failed 3D-conversion (e.g. a broadcasting error), some artists are left in the 3D subclass without the 3D-specific attributes (e.g. AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d'), causing persistent failures until the interpreter/kernel is restarted. The wrong assumption is that it is safe to set obj.__class__ to the 3D subclass before the 3D-specific setup succeeds.
- Root cause (file, class, method): lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py — conversion helpers such as line_2d_to_3d / text_2d_to_3d / line_collection_2d_to_3d / patch_2d_to_3d / pathpatch_2d_to_3d / patch_collection_2d_to_3d / poly_collection_2d_to_3d set obj.__class__ to the 3D class and then call set_3d_properties (or set_segments / set_verts_and_codes). If set_3d_properties raises (e.g. np.broadcast_to inside Line3D.set_3d_properties), the instance is left in the 3D class without expected attributes like _verts3d.
- Proposed fix: In each conversion helper, call the 3D-class setup methods as unbound methods on the original instance first (e.g. Line3D.set_3d_properties(line, zs, zdir)); only if that succeeds, set obj.__class__ = Target3DClass. Apply this consistently to all locations that currently reassign __class__ before setup.
- Hardcoded values/constants: none to change. The bug is about ordering of operations; formulas remain the same (preserve exact formula: zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs)); self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)).
- Methods to change/override: do not need new methods — change ordering in existing helper functions in art3d.py (text_2d_to_3d, line_2d_to_3d, line_collection_2d_to_3d, patch_2d_to_3d, pathpatch_2d_to_3d, patch_collection_2d_to_3d, poly_collection_2d_to_3d). No new class methods required.
- Investigation gaps: the agent did not run a concrete repro in the repository to reproduce the half-conversion and verify that calling the target-class unbound methods is safe in every edge-case (e.g. side effects or attributes those methods assume). Add tests that simulate the failing broadcast and assert no partially-converted objects remain.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Partial conversion leaves object in inconsistent class
What: art3d conversion helpers assign obj.__class__ = <3DClass> then call setup (e.g. set_3d_properties). If setup raises, the instance stays in the 3D class but lacks attributes the 3D class expects (e.g. _verts3d). Why it matters: causes AttributeError during draw later and persists across runs in the same process.

### Finding 2: Line3D.set_3d_properties can raise during conversion
What: Line3D.set_3d_properties uses broadcasting that can raise (np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs))); when it raises, conversion helper had already switched class. Why it matters: broadcasting failures (user's first-run error "input operand has more dimensions than allowed by the axis remapping") are the exact trigger that leaves an invalid object behind.

  zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs))
  self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
(cp: get_file_context lines ~171–176).

### Finding 3: All __class__ reassignments discovered (must be fixed consistently)
What: Search found multiple places that do obj.__class__ = Something followed by setup call — all need the same reordering to avoid the same failure mode.
Locations & source checkpoint: search_codebase for "__class__ = " returned exact hits (cp: search_codebase observation): lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py at lines 155, 224, 318, 382, 392, 655, 880 (functions: text_2d_to_3d, line_2d_to_3d, line_collection_2d_to_3d, patch_2d_to_3d, pathpatch_2d_to_3d, patch_collection_2d_to_3d, poly_collection_2d_to_3d). Why it matters: fix must be applied to all these places.

### Finding 4: Safe ordering fix — call unbound setup first, then switch __class__
What: Instead of setting __class__ then calling set_3d_properties, call TargetClass.set_3d_properties(obj, ...) first, then obj.__class__ = TargetClass. Why it matters: if setup raises, obj remains in original class and will not break draw later. Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify the draft enhanced report's diagnostic claims against the actual source in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py and related callers. I used get_file_context to read art3d.py and axes3d.py and search_codebase to find caller locations. Below I list what I looked for, why, what I expected to learn, and what the code shows.

A. Tools used and why
- I fetched lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py (get_file_context) to inspect:
  - the 2D->3D conversion helper functions (text_2d_to_3d, line_2d_to_3d, line_collection_2d_to_3d, patch_2d_to_3d, pathpatch_2d_to_3d, patch_collection_2d_to_3d, poly_collection_2d_to_3d) to confirm whether they reassign __class__ before calling setup methods.
  - Line3D.set_3d_properties implementation to check for np.broadcast_to usage and assignment of _verts3d.
  - draw methods that read _verts3d to confirm that missing _verts3d would trigger AttributeError during draw.
  - other 3D setup methods to see if they perform broadcasting that could raise.
- I fetched lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axes3d.py (get_file_context) to confirm how axes3d.plot invokes conversion helpers and whether axes3d.plot itself performs broadcast_to that could raise earlier in the flow.
- I used search_codebase to find callers of line_2d_to_3d.

B. What the code shows (line references below are from the file dump I opened)

1) Conversion helpers reassign __class__ before running 3D setup
- text_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 153–156:
    - obj.__class__ = Text3D
    - obj.set_3d_properties(z, zdir)
  - This confirms the conversion helper switches the instance __class__ and then calls the 3D setup method.

- line_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 221–226:
    - line.__class__ = Line3D
    - line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)

- line_collection_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 315–320:
    - segments3d = _paths_to_3d_segments(col.get_paths(), zs, zdir)
    - col.__class__ = Line3DCollection
    - col.set_segments(segments3d)

- patch_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 379–384:
    - verts = _get_patch_verts(patch)
    - patch.__class__ = Patch3D
    - patch.set_3d_properties(verts, z, zdir)

- pathpatch_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 386–393:
    - mpath = trans.transform_path(path)
    - pathpatch.__class__ = PathPatch3D
    - pathpatch.set_3d_properties(mpath, z, zdir)

- patch_collection_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 636–661:
    - if isinstance(col, PathCollection):
        col.__class__ = Path3DCollection
      elif isinstance(col, PatchCollection):
        col.__class__ = Patch3DCollection
    - col.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)

- poly_collection_2d_to_3d:
  - lines 876–883:
    - segments_3d, codes = _paths_to_3d_segments_with_codes(col.get_paths(), zs, zdir)
    - col.__class__ = Poly3DCollection
    - col.set_verts_and_codes(segments_3d, codes)
    - col.set_3d_properties()

These occurrences confirm the draft's core observation: many conversion helpers change obj.__class__ to the 3D subclass and then call a 3D-specific setup method.

2) set_3d_properties and other setup methods use broadcasting that can raise
- Line3D.set_3d_properties:
  - lines 171–176:
    - xs = self.get_xdata()
    - ys = self.get_ydata()
    - zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs))
    - self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
  - This shows the exact expression that can raise (np.broadcast_to) and the assignment of self._verts3d that the 3D draw path expects.

- Patch3D.set_3d_properties:
  - lines 331–334:
    - zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(verts))
    - self._segment3d = [juggle_axes(...) for ((x, y), z) in zip(verts, zs)]
  - _path_to_3d_segment and related helpers also use np.broadcast_to (e.g., lines 231, 255, 272).

- axes3d.plot itself uses np.broadcast_to:
  - line 1280: zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, np.shape(xs))
  - then at 1282–1284: lines = super().plot(xs, ys, ...); for line in lines: art3d.line_2d_to_3d(line, zs=zs, zdir=zdir)
  - This means broadcasting can happen at the axes3d.plot entry point (before the conversion helper is called), and also inside some setup methods later.

3) draw reads _verts3d and will raise if missing
- Line3D.draw:
  - lines 213–217:
    - xs3d, ys3d, zs3d = self._verts3d
    - xs, ys, zs = proj3d.proj_transform(xs3d, ys3d, zs3d, self.axes.M)
    - self.set_data(xs, ys)
  - If _verts3d is not set (AttributeError), draw will raise exactly as in the user's trace (AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d').

4) Which conversions are actually vulnerable to the "partial conversion" failure mode?
I verified the ordering and broadcasting points above and I note these important nuances:

- For a persistent "partial conversion" (object left in 3D class but without required attributes), two things must happen:
  a) __class__ must be reassigned to the 3D subclass; and
  b) the subsequent 3D setup invocation (set_3d_properties, set_segments, set_verts_and_codes, etc.) must raise before it initializes the attributes the 3D class expects.
- Not all conversions will have the broadcasting call execute after __class__ reassignment. Example:
  - line_collection_2d_to_3d computes segments3d via _paths_to_3d_segments(...) before assigning col.__class__ (see lines 315–318), so a broadcasting error inside _paths_to_3d_segments would occur before col.__class__ is changed — in that case no partial conversion is left behind.
  - Conversely, line_2d_to_3d assigns line.__class__ = Line3D and then calls line.set_3d_properties, which does np.broadcast_to; if that broadcast raises, the Line2D instance is left as Line3D without _verts3d and is vulnerable to the later AttributeError on draw.
- Several setup methods do use np.broadcast_to (Line3D.set_3d_properties, Patch3D.set_3d_properties, _paths_to_3d_segments/_with_codes, poly collection helpers). Any exception raised by these setup calls after __class__ reassignment can create the broken state.

5) Are all claims in the draft supported?
- Supported claims:
  - Conversion helpers reassigning __class__ before setup: confirmed at the locations listed in the draft (art3d.py).
  - The Line3D.set_3d_properties broadcasting expression and subsequent assignment to self._verts3d: confirmed (lines 171–176).
  - Draw expects _verts3d and will raise AttributeError if it is missing: confirmed (lines 213–217).
  - The problem can be persistent across runs because once an instance's __class__ is changed in-process, exceptions during setup can leave it in that mutated state until the process restarts: this follows directly from the observed code pattern and Python object semantics (confirmed by code paths).
- Claims that need nuance or softening:
  - The draft implies all listed conversion helpers are equally likely to leave objects partially converted after a broadcasting error. The code shows some helpers compute potentially-buggy data (e.g., converting paths to 3D segments) before reassigning __class__ (so those particular broadcast failures would not cause partial conversion). I therefore refined the statement to say: all of these helpers share the risky pattern (reassign then setup), but whether a particular broadcast will leave a partially converted object depends on whether the broadcast happens before or after the __class__ change in that helper.
  - The draft's "Investigation gap / Proposed fix" statements belong outside the RootCause (they are about remediation and unverified assumptions). Per instructions, the final report must be a pure diagnosis. I will remove prescriptive text from RootCause and mark the unverified test assumption as an evidence/note outside the RootCause.

6) Investigation analysis completeness check (other locations / caller flow)
- axes3d.plot performs a broadcast (line 1280) before super().plot and before calling art3d.line_2d_to_3d (line 1284). If that broadcast raises, conversion helpers won't be called and no partial conversion occurs. This is an important distinction when reproducing the user's initial broadcasting error: the broadcast could have occurred in axes3d.plot (pre-conversion) or inside Line3D.set_3d_properties (post-conversion). The draft's reproduction steps should allow both possibilities; I adjusted wording to be precise that the persistent AttributeError arises when the exception occurs after a conversion helper has reassigned __class__.

- I checked for other occurrences of "__class__ =" in the repository for art3d conversion helpers; the locations the draft listed match what I found in art3d.py at the line numbers cited.

7) Field discipline check — mapping content to fields
- Description: the draft's Description is mostly correct (it describes the problem), but it included a general "conversion helper can fail" — I'll keep it concise and code-backed.
- RootCause: must be limited to the incorrect assumptions and the locations that encode them. I reworked RootCause to list the exact helpers and the exact risky pattern and to show the specific broadcasting/formula lines that can raise (preserving the formula detail, as requested).
- ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior: the draft's wording is fine and I preserved the user-visible behavior wording only.
- StepsToReproduce: I kept user-level steps but made them explicit about triggering the broadcast that fails after class reassignment versus before it.

8) Missing or extra findings in the draft
- The draft included a Proposed fix and a note about an "Investigation gap" (whether calling unbound target-class setup methods is safe). Per the instructions, the final report must not prescribe fixes; I removed the proposed fix from the RootCause and noted the gap as an evidence item (untested assumption) rather than as part of RootCause.
- I added the nuance about axes3d.plot broadcasting (it can raise before conversion helpers are called) because that affects whether a broadcast error will produce a persistent partial conversion; that nuance was not explicit in the draft and is supported by code (line 1280).

Summary of verification (code quotes)
- Conversion helpers change __class__ then call setup (examples):
  - text_2d_to_3d (art3d.py:153–156): obj.__class__ = Text3D; obj.set_3d_properties(z, zdir)
  - line_2d_to_3d (art3d.py:221–225): line.__class__ = Line3D; line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)
  - line_collection_2d_to_3d (315–319): segments3d = ...; col.__class__ = Line3DCollection; col.set_segments(segments3d)
  - patch_2d_to_3d (379–383): patch.__class__ = Patch3D; patch.set_3d_properties(verts, z, zdir)
  - pathpatch_2d_to_3d (391–393): pathpatch.__class__ = PathPatch3D; pathpatch.set_3d_properties(...)
  - patch_collection_2d_to_3d (654–661) and poly_collection_2d_to_3d (876–883) similarly.
- Line3D.set_3d_properties contains the broadcasting expression and then sets _verts3d (art3d.py:171–176):
  - zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs))
  - self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
- Line3D.draw expects self._verts3d (art3d.py:213–216) and will raise AttributeError if it is absent.

Conclusion of Phase 1
- The draft's central diagnosis is supported: several conversion helpers change an instance's __class__ to a 3D subclass and then call setup methods that can raise (notably via np.broadcast_to), and if those setup calls raise, the instance can be left in the 3D class without the attributes the class expects (e.g., _verts3d), causing an AttributeError at draw that persists in-process.
- I refined the RootCause wording to avoid overclaiming (some helpers compute broadcastable data before switching __class__, and axes3d.plot may broadcast earlier), and I removed prescription language and the unverified fix suggestion from the RootCause.
